perturbation.original_text: h) "BERKSHIRE PARTIES" is Berkshire, and any affiliates of Berkshire, as defined herein. For purposes of this Agreement, "AFFILIATES" means any other person or entity that directly or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by or is under common control with, Berkshire.
perturbation.changed_text: h) "BERKSHIRE PARTIES" is Berkshire, and any affiliates of Berkshire, as defined herein. For purposes of this Agreement, "AFFILIATES" means any other person or entity that directly or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by or is under common control with, Berkshire. However, for the purposes of Clause 2, 'AFFILIATES' shall also include any entity with which Berkshire has a marketing agreement, regardless of control.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by creating two definitions for 'AFFILIATES'. The primary definition in Section 1(h) focuses on control. However, the added stipulation in Section 1(h) expands the definition for Clause 2, encompassing entities with mere marketing agreements, irrespective of control. This contradicts the intended scope of 'AFFILIATES' as initially defined, particularly in other sections such as Section 2(c) which stipulates the License Rights are exclusive to Berkshire and may not be assigned or in any way conveyed by Berkshire without Auriemma's express written consent, except in the event of a merger by Berkshire with another entity offering Banking Services and by adding the additional condition this create uncertainty on who is an affiliate when the clause is dealing with 'License Rights'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. ENDORSEMENT AND GRANT OF RIGHTS. During the Contract Period: a) Subject to the terms of Paragraph 6 below, Auriemma will make the appearances and provide to Berkshire during the Services Period the services, initiatives and programs described in Schedule A attached hereto (the "Endorsement Services"); and b) Auriemma grants to Berkshire the exclusive right and license (the "License Rights") to use the Auriemma Identification during the Contract Period and throughout the Contract Territory solely in connection with the advertisement and promotion of Berkshire and the Financial Services; c) The License Rights are exclusive to Berkshire and may not be assigned or in any way conveyed by Berkshire without Auriemma's express written consent, except in the event of a merger by Berkshire with another entity offering Banking Services.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction by defining 'AFFILIATES' differently for Clause 2 than for the rest of the agreement. The original text defines 'AFFILIATES' based on control, while the changed text expands this definition for Clause 2 to include entities with mere marketing agreements, regardless of control. This contradicts the meaning of 'AFFILIATES' in Section 2(c), which states that "The License Rights are exclusive to Berkshire" and are restricted in assignment, only making exception in the case of "a merger by Berkshire with another entity offering Banking Services". The change introduces uncertainty on whether 'License Rights' extend to entities merely having a marketing agreement with Berkshire, thus creating an inconsistency within the document.

perturbation.original_text: Past due cash payments (i.e., payments due more than thirty (30) days after Berkshire's receipt of the applicable invoice) shall bear interest at the rate of one (1%) percent per month.
perturbation.changed_text: Past due cash payments (i.e., payments due more than sixty (60) days after Berkshire's receipt of the applicable invoice) shall bear interest at the rate of one (1%) percent per month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the number of days before interest accrues from 30 days to 60 days directly contradicts the payment terms outlined in Section 10(a). This section states that the agreement can be terminated if a payment is not made within ten (10) days after written notice of failure to make payment, while the current clause grants a 60-day window before late fees incur. This discrepancy creates uncertainty around when Berkshire is considered in breach of contract for late payments.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. TERMINATION AND DEFAULT. a) TERMINATION FOR BREACH. Either Party shall have the right, without prejudice to any other rights it may have, to terminate this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches its obligation hereunder and such breach remains uncured. A material breach occurs if either Party (i) fails to make any payment, or (ii) fails to observe or perform any of the covenants, agreements, or obligations (other than payments of money). Upon the breach of either of the above conditions, the non-defaulting party may terminate this Agreement as follows: (A) as to a default under clause (i) above, if payment is not made within ten (10) days after the defaulting party shall have received written notice of such failure to make payment; or (B) as to a default under clause (ii) above, if such default is not cured within thirty (30) days after the defaulting party shall have received written notice specifying in reasonable detail the nature of such default and such action the defaulting party must take in order to cure each such item of default.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the grace period for payments before interest accrues from 30 days to 60 days. This directly contradicts Section 10(a), which allows for contract termination if payment is not made within 10 days of written notice. This creates a clear inconsistency because the 60-day window provided in the Changed Text conflicts with the 10-day cure period in Section 10(a), making it unclear when Berkshire is in breach. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' where 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties.'

perturbation.original_text: All notices required hereunder shall be sent by overnight mail or first class mail, or by confirmed electronic mail to the parties at the following addresses, or such other addresses as the parties may designate in writing to each other from time to time:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices required hereunder shall be sent by overnight mail or first class mail. However, notices regarding termination of the agreement may be sent via carrier pigeon, to the parties at the following addresses, or such other addresses as the parties may designate in writing to each other from time to time:
perturbation.explanation: By stipulating that notices regarding the termination of the agreement may be sent via carrier pigeon, it creates contradiction and ambiguity with the existing statement about overnight mail, first class mail or confirmed electronic mail. The enforceability of a termination notice delivered by carrier pigeon is questionable, especially given the need for prompt and reliable communication in legal matters and the lack of confirmation. This introduces uncertainty and contradicts the clear intent of the original notice clause, making it difficult to determine whether a valid termination notice has been served.
perturbation.contradicted_text:
14. NOTICES. All notices required hereunder shall be sent by overnight mail or first class mail. However, notices regarding termination of the agreement may be sent via carrier pigeon, to the parties at the following addresses, or such other addresses as the parties may designate in writing to each other from time to time:

If to Auriemma: Geno Auriemma
With a copy to: Kahan, Kerensky & Capossela, LLP
Attn: Sol Kerensky & Justin L. Murphy
45 Hartford Turnpike
Vernon, CT 06066

If to Berkshire: Berkshire Bank
Attn:Sean A. Gray, EVP â€” Retail Banking

With a copy to: Berkshire Bank

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by adding 'carrier pigeon' as an acceptable method for delivering termination notices. The original text implies a reasonable expectation of timely and verifiable delivery using 'overnight mail or first class mail'. Allowing carrier pigeon contradicts this expectation, introducing uncertainty and potentially rendering termination notices unenforceable due to unreliable delivery and lack of confirmation. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition, as the different sections of the contract provide conflicting methods for delivering notices, leading to disputes over contractual obligations, therefore it's a 'YES'.
